blurb: Bigelow's tansyaster throws big lavender-purple, gold-eyed daisies on branching stems from late summer into fall, when pollinators need them most. A short-lived, self-sowing native of dry, sunny ground that drifts politely through a xeric bed.
spread: Clumping and short-lived (biennial to short-lived perennial); self-sows to persist
seasons: Grayish foliage; big lavender-purple, gold-eyed daisies from late summer into fall
wildlife: Important late-season nectar for native bees and butterflies; a specialist-bee (tansyaster) host

care.hardiness: A tough Southern-Rocky-Mountain native (W. Colorado south to New Mexico and Arizona) of dry, sunny ground, fully cold-hardy through Boulder's zone 5b–6a winters.[1][2] It behaves as a biennial or short-lived perennial — individual plants are not long-lived, so it persists in the garden by self-sowing.[1]
care.planting: Set out container-grown plants in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or start it from seed (the more natural way for a self-sower). Site it in lean, sharply drained ground in full sun and water in to establish; once rooted it asks for almost nothing. In pots use a gritty, fast-draining mix and protect from winter wet, which rots the crown — but because it is short-lived and seeds itself, it is happiest in the open ground.[2]
care.sun: Full sun — it is a plant of open, dry slopes and disturbed sunny ground, and our intense high-altitude sun suits it.[1][2]
care.soil: Lean, dry, sharply drained sandy or gravelly soils; it tolerates Boulder's alkaline clay so long as drainage is good. Avoid rich, amended, or wet ground, which shortens its life and invites crown rot.[2]
care.water: Low; xeric once established. Give occasional deep soakings the first season to root it in, then little to no supplemental water — it is adapted to a semi-arid climate and resents summer wet.[2]
care.spacing: Allow about 12–18 in. between plants; each forms a branching, open clump 1–3 ft tall.[1][2]
care.propagation: By seed is the natural method — it does not divide. Sow the ripe seed in fall and let winter cold-stratify it, or surface-sow shallowly in spring after a light cold-moist chill; the fine seed wants light to germinate, so barely cover it. There is no reliable vegetative method; keep the planting going by letting it self-sow.[1][2]
care.sow: Sow seed shallowly in fall to overwinter and stratify naturally, or surface-sow in spring; press into the surface and barely cover, as the seed needs light. Seedlings appear as basal rosettes that bloom the following year.[1][2]
care.stratify: A light cold-moist period improves and evens germination — fall-sown seed stratifies naturally over winter, or chill seed moist for a few weeks before a spring sowing.[1][2]
care.bloom: Blooms late summer into fall — roughly August through October — with big lavender-purple, gold-eyed daisies, exactly when most of the garden is finishing and pollinators are short of forage.[1]
care.maintenance: Very low care: leave it alone once established and avoid overwatering or feeding, which makes it floppy and short-lived. Do not deadhead everything — let some seed ripen and scatter so the planting renews itself.[1][2]
care.selfsow: Because plants are short-lived (biennial to short-lived perennial), self-sowing is how it persists — let a portion of the seedheads shatter rather than deadheading them all, and volunteers will fill in. It drifts politely in a xeric bed rather than running, and surplus rosettes are easy to thin.[1]
care.troubles: Few problems in a lean, sunny, well-drained site; crown and root rot in rich, wet, or over-irrigated soil is the main risk, otherwise largely pest- and disease-free.[2]
care.companions: Other xeric Front-Range natives that share its hot, lean, full-sun ground — dotted gayfeather (*Liatris punctata*), sulphur flower buckwheat (*Eriogonum umbellatum*), blue grama, and the asters — for a late-season pollinator drift.[1]

edible.summary: No documented culinary use; grown as a native ornamental and late-season pollinator plant, not for eating.[1]
edible.caution: Tansyaster has no documented food use, so it is treated as inedible — don't eat any part. As an aster-family (Asteraceae) native it is not known to be notably poisonous, but absence of documented edibility is not a green light to eat it.[1][2]
